chapters.title: Lead Like Lives Depend On It

articles.title: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Convey appoints Jim Hansen as Chief Product Officer and Britta Hormann as Chief of Staff to strengthen executive leadership team  Business Wire

articles.title: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure - SDxCentral
articles.snippet: Ciena bolsters executive team with supply chain, product appointments to scale AI infrastructure  SDxCentral

articles.title: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue - FinTech Global
articles.snippet: Nair joins Fenergo executive team to scale global revenue  FinTech Global

post1.text:
Most companies scaling past $50M hire executives to solve problems. The best ones hire executives to prevent them.

There is a meaningful difference, and it shows up fast.

Recent moves across the business world show companies aggressively bolstering leadership teams with specialized C-suite and senior roles, not because things are broken, but because the complexity ahead demands it. That is proactive leadership architecture.

Three things scaling companies get wrong when building executive teams:

1. They hire for the company they are, not the company they are becoming. A leader who thrived at $30M may not have the range for $150M.

2. They confuse activity with accountability. Titles without clearly scoped ownership create expensive confusion at the top.

3. They treat executive hiring as a reaction to pain rather than an investment in growth velocity.

Leadership at scale is less about individual brilliance and more about designing a team that can carry more weight than any one person could.

What is the one leadership gap your company is tolerating right now that will cost you at the next inflection point?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SDxCentral reported that Ciena has expanded its executive team with focused supply chain and product leadership appointments, specifically to support scaling its AI infrastructure business.

This is exactly what scaling looks like in practice.

At Autobahn, we work with companies pushing from $50M toward $500M, and the pattern is consistent. The organizations that scale without breaking are the ones that get ahead of leadership gaps before the gaps become crises. They do not wait until a function is underwater to put a strong leader in it.

Ciena is not adding headcount. They are adding capacity at the top. That distinction matters enormously.

If you are building toward your next revenue milestone, ask yourself whether your leadership structure today can actually carry you there.

Read the full story here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMixwFBVV95cUxOQm05NW1RSUh0Uk1fa0hYMU9QTHVnaXJhZEpfdVVjaE9uWURQS3QtNGxCMWZhWjNwdWUyeXFwengxbDZZRFd6OF91ZTdtUmtNSGoyTkRzdklfeENYQ09ITWN6UjhWQVE0UE5sQmx3MXZJdmNjLVhSM2plUXl5aXhyOS1aS2hCb2xYQVU4UUdDakphV3NPWTdfazVQVWJtUEs2bFJWY3VzeTk5N2tVN3dFdE5TOFlqMUZxaWZvdTRfQ2w2QmVOUFF3?oc=5

post3.text:
Companies across every industry are making bold executive team moves right now. New Chief Product Officers. New Chiefs of Staff. New revenue leadership. The message is clear: scaling requires intentional leadership design.

Chapter 1 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business: Navigating the Road from $50 Million to $500 Million and Beyond, is titled Lead Like Lives Depend On It. Written by Katherine Slain, this chapter confronts something most scaling companies avoid saying out loud: the leadership behaviors that got you here will actively work against you if you do not evolve them.

Katherine draws a sharp line between leaders who manage the business in front of them and leaders who build for the business ahead. The distinction is not subtle. And at scale, it is the difference between momentum and stall.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Adding executives is easy. Building a leadership team that can actually scale your company from $50M to $500M is something else entirely. Most growing companies confuse the two until a critical moment exposes the gap. The companies pulling ahead right now share one trait: they treat leadership architecture as a growth strategy, not an HR function. Here is what that looks like in practice.
posts.email_subject: Your leadership team is already too small

chapters.title: Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit

articles.title: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success - Harlem World Magazine
articles.snippet: Interview: Harlem Local Darius Ross, From Homelessness To Entrepreneurial Success  Harlem World Magazine

articles.title: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business - U.S. Chamber of Commerce
articles.snippet: How to Create an Exit Strategy for Your Business  U.S. Chamber of Commerce

articles.title: From startup to exit: Deepak Hathiramani’s playbook for GovCon entrepreneurs - The American Bazaar
articles.snippet: From startup to exit: Deepak Hathiramani’s playbook for GovCon entrepreneurs  The American Bazaar

post1.text:
Most business owners think they build value, then sell. The reality is backwards. The way you build determines whether you have anything worth selling.

The U.S. Chamber of Commerce recently outlined how exit strategy shapes every major business decision long before a transaction is on the table. That framing is exactly right, and most $50M+ leaders miss it.

Here is what actually moves your multiple:

First, recurring revenue is not just a business model preference. It is a valuation multiplier. Buyers pay a premium for predictability, and they discount chaos at the same rate.

Second, owner dependency is a liability on your cap table. If the business cannot operate without you for 90 days, acquirers will price that risk into their offer.

Third, timing your exit around market conditions matters less than having a clean house when opportunity knocks. Most deals fall apart in due diligence, not negotiation.

You cannot optimize what you have not measured. So here is the question: do you know what your business would actually sell for today?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The American Bazaar profiles Deepak Hathiramani's journey from building a government contracting firm to executing a successful exit. His story is a clear illustration of something we see constantly with scaling companies: the entrepreneurs who get the best outcomes are the ones who treat the exit as a destination they have been navigating toward for years, not a finish line they stumble across.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders in the $50M to $500M range who are often surprised to learn how much runway they need to genuinely maximize their multiple. Cleaning up financials, reducing concentration risk, and building leadership depth are not pre-sale tasks. They are growth tasks that pay off at closing.

If you are building with an exit in mind, this story is worth your time.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMipwFBVV95cUxQZmViR0NLelR2NGl2Ui1iamZ4TS02MTNWcmpELVkwTDBpankxVW5zQko5dHg0UndfQnFHRXZtZGJhN3FPVkE3Tl8zZWRlRXJBU0cyY2UyakpPd1ZmZzNMU2tFOHZoV1RrNXVOZi1TclBOM0pMVFZSNUozcFZHTWtPdmljdm5NcDZyY0Q2NDc1eV9yU2dnSVN3SFZlckg2RF8ydlg1ZGVQYw

post3.text:
The U.S. Chamber of Commerce recently published a guide on building an exit strategy for your business. It covers the basics well. But for companies operating between $50M and $500M, the basics are not the problem.

Chapter 2 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, tackles exactly this gap. Titled Maximize Your Multiple to Achieve Your Exit, it was written by Paul Belair and George Hohman, two practitioners who have sat across the table in real transactions. Their core argument is that the decisions you make three to five years before a sale do more to shape your outcome than anything you negotiate on the day you sign.

One thing they make clear: multiple expansion is not luck. It is a set of deliberate, measurable actions that most owners simply do not know to take until it is too late to take them.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most business owners think about their exit when they are ready to leave. That is already too late. The multiple you receive is not determined at the negotiating table. It is determined by decisions you made years earlier about revenue predictability, leadership depth, and operational independence. Here is what actually drives valuation for companies between $50M and $500M.
posts.email_subject: What your business is actually worth today

chapters.title: What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes

articles.title: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility - TradingView
articles.snippet: Uniserve Advances National Digital Infrastructure Strategy with $1.8 Million Committed Growth Credit Facility  TradingView

articles.title: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan - CUInsight
articles.snippet: From paper to performance: How to execute your 2026 credit union strategic plan  CUInsight

articles.title: Strategy Update and 2025 Preliminary Results - WPP
articles.snippet: Strategy Update and 2025 Preliminary Results  WPP

post1.text:
Most strategic plans fail not because the strategy was wrong, but because execution never had a real owner.

WPP's recent strategy update and preliminary results announcement is a reminder that even global firms struggle when strategy and execution live in separate rooms. The companies that scale past $100M are the ones that close that gap.

Here is what leaders at $50M+ companies consistently get wrong:

1. Strategy is built in a room and handed down. The people executing it had no part in building it, so they have no stake in it.

2. Priorities multiply. When everything is a priority, nothing moves. Scaling companies need a short list they actually protect.

3. Accountability gets polite. Leaders review progress in meetings but avoid the real conversation when something is off track. Politeness kills momentum.

Execution is not a department. It is a discipline that has to be designed into how your leadership team operates every single week.

What is the one thing your team agreed to prioritize this quarter that has quietly slipped to the back burner?

post2.text:
A recent piece in CUInsight tackled a challenge every leadership team knows too well: the gap between a polished strategic plan and actually executing it. The article focused on credit unions preparing for 2026, but the core problem applies to any company pushing past $50M.

At Autobahn, we see this constantly. Companies invest serious time building a plan, then return to day-to-day operations and wonder six months later why the needle has not moved.

The fix is not a better document. It is a better operating rhythm. Weekly accountability, clear owners for each priority, and leaders who are willing to have honest conversations when execution stalls.

If your 2025 plan is already collecting dust, this article is worth five minutes of your time.

Read it here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiqAFBVV95cUxOWVRBZ0VjRlV6U3JLUnBwLTdLeWktRDczdDFWaUMzRHdid0ZCQmhtdWY2dERjZi1UNVg5S0R0VXlua3N0MDJSYUNzeEJiMC1ibXhTa3dpVlNaRG54YUVCRzhYWVUydXNGLXI3VHljVEFWb3VNVkNvUHpRWTE4a0JSRU0tcEtrRGNfRHVyRDVxTWNmRnBLb1dVMmVZXzUzODI3SFNGX09jR2U

post3.text:
Strategic plans look great on slides. Then real life shows up.

In Chapter 3 of Rock Your Business, titled "What We Learned Selling Fish Food and Nike Shoes," authors Rafa Loyo and Jim Randall draw on two very different business experiences to show what separates companies that execute from companies that just plan.

One of the sharpest insights in the chapter is that the businesses that scale are not the ones with the most sophisticated strategies. They are the ones that build simple, repeatable execution habits and hold the line on them, even when growth gets messy.

It sounds obvious. Almost no one actually does it.

WPP's recent public strategy update is a reminder that even organizations with enormous resources have to answer the same question every $50M company faces: how do you keep strategy alive after the meeting ends?

Rock Your Business, the follow-up to Rock the Recession, answers that question with hard-won experience. Rock Your Business — available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your strategic plan is not failing because your strategy is wrong. It is failing because execution was never designed into how your team operates. At the $50M to $500M growth stage, the gap between planning and doing is where companies stall or break. Here is what the businesses that actually scale do differently.
posts.email_subject: Why your strategy isn't the problem

chapters.title: Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation

articles.title: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene - Gartner
articles.snippet: AI Time Savings Won’t Drive Revenue Unless CSOs Intervene  Gartner

articles.title: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive - abasto.com
articles.snippet: B2B Sales Strategy Now Demands AI, Personalization to Survive  abasto.com

articles.title: Growth amid uncertainty: Jump-starting B2B sales performance - McKinsey & Company
articles.snippet: Growth amid uncertainty: Jump-starting B2B sales performance  McKinsey & Company

post1.text:
Most companies at $50M don't have a sales strategy. They have a collection of habits that worked when the business was smaller.

There's a difference. And that difference becomes expensive fast.

Gartner recently found that even as AI creates significant time savings for sales teams, those gains don't convert to revenue without intentional leadership intervention. In other words, efficiency without direction is just faster spinning in place.

Here's what that means for leaders scaling past $50M:

First, activity is not strategy. More calls, more demos, more pipeline meetings are not a plan. They are noise without a clear targeting framework.

Second, your best rep's instincts are not repeatable. What works in one deal needs to be extracted, systematized, and taught.

Third, McKinsey's research on B2B sales growth points to one consistent pattern among outperformers: they define their sales motion before they scale headcount.

You cannot hire your way out of a strategy gap.

So here's the question: If your top salesperson left tomorrow, would your revenue follow them out the door?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Gartner makes something very clear: AI is saving sales teams real time, but that time is evaporating without revenue to show for it. The reason, according to Gartner, is that chief sales officers are not stepping in to redirect those gains toward growth.

At Autobahn, we see this play out constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. Leadership invests in tools, technology, and headcount, but without a deliberate sales strategy underneath it all, the investment leaks.

Efficiency without direction is not a competitive advantage. It is a well-organized drift.

If your sales team is doing more but growing less, the problem is not effort. The problem is structure.

Read the Gartner piece and then ask yourself honestly: does your sales leader have a real strategy, or a really good routine?

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiYEFVX3lxTE83YmJZYVNxUUtqV1lJanRha3hGWnFtdjJYSm5FWURzTUFrVm1uTDB2R1VRWklWVlM4cVhURDNTMVFxVmw0Ql9YZUZrdndhcC1JWFRZczVJRmxlR1REQmVvMg?oc=5

post3.text:
McKinsey just released new research showing that B2B companies can drive meaningful sales growth even in uncertain markets. The key is not more spending. It is sharper sales architecture.

Chapter 4 of our upcoming book, Rock Your Business, hits this directly. The chapter is titled 'Every Day Without a Real Sales Strategy is a Donation,' and it is authored by Deb and Brandon, two practitioners who have built and rebuilt sales engines inside companies navigating serious growth.

The insight that stops leaders cold: most scaling companies are not losing deals to competitors. They are losing revenue to their own undefined process. The chapter gives you a framework for diagnosing exactly where the leak is and how to seal it before the next growth phase begins.

This is not theory. It is the work.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Your company crossed $50M in revenue and your sales team is busier than ever. So why does growth feel harder than it should? The answer almost nobody wants to hear: you scaled activity before you built a strategy. Every quarter without a defined sales architecture is not a plateau. It is a choice to donate margin to the market.
posts.email_subject: Your sales team is busy. Is it working?

chapters.title: What Does Your Marketing Smell Like?

articles.title: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture - Fierce Pharma
articles.snippet: Big Pharma’s Big Brand: Inside Eli Lilly’s marketing culture  Fierce Pharma

articles.title: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns - Brand Innovators
articles.snippet: Why JBL is building marketing around culture not campaigns  Brand Innovators

articles.title: Why Culture Is a Business Investment, Not a Brand Expense - AdvisorHub
articles.snippet: Why Culture Is a Business Investment, Not a Brand Expense  AdvisorHub

post1.text:
Your brand is not your logo. It is not your tagline. It is not the color palette your agency charged you $40,000 to develop.

Your brand is what your company smells like when no one is watching.

JBL recently made a deliberate shift away from campaign-driven marketing toward building around culture. The result is a brand identity that holds up across markets, channels, and customer touchpoints without needing a refresh every 18 months.

For companies scaling past $50M, this distinction matters enormously. Three things worth considering:

1. Campaigns are rented attention. Culture is owned equity. One disappears when the budget runs out.

2. Inconsistency at scale is expensive. When your brand means something internally, it costs less to communicate externally.

3. Marketing that emerges from culture does not need to be manufactured. It is already true.

Most leadership teams treat branding as a marketing problem. The ones who scale fastest treat it as an operational one.

What does your company actually stand for when no one is pitching anything?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Brand Innovators caught our attention. JBL is deliberately moving away from one-off campaigns and building its entire marketing approach around cultural identity instead.

For companies in the $50M to $500M range, this is not just an interesting case study. It is a warning.

At that stage of growth, your marketing infrastructure is being stress-tested for the first time. If your brand only lives inside a campaign, it will not survive the pressure of new markets, new hires, and faster execution cycles.

Culture-led marketing creates internal alignment first. That alignment becomes your most durable competitive advantage externally.

If your team cannot describe what your brand stands for without pulling up a slide deck, that is the problem to solve before the next campaign launches.

Read the full article here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMikgFBVV95cUxPbXpYamE0dkF6MWVkcEkwUHlnSHk5bUtIbmV4ZTlDNEk3MEpURWZDdUo5b1Q4aTMtZkIzUy1scXU3VEJUREY2Vks0NjFGV0s2QXYtejBPMm93NlRrSC00MHRSdlpQcnJqeW00akp4eFRLeTZwRGxOV1BEQmR0eEpoVXB0cFpPaUhVeTBtU3E3WGxfZw

post3.text:
There is a chapter in our upcoming book that asks a question most executives have never considered about their own company.

What does your marketing smell like?

Chapter 5 of Rock Your Business, written by Kayli Simpson and Yasmin West, tackles branding and marketing in a way that is built specifically for companies navigating serious growth. Not startup advice. Not enterprise theory. The real friction that shows up when a $50M company tries to market like a $500M one before the foundation is ready.

One thing Kayli and Yasmin make clear: most companies at this stage are not suffering from a lack of marketing activity. They are suffering from a lack of marketing identity. Those are two very different problems that require very different solutions.

The distinction they draw will change how you look at every dollar in your marketing budget.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most companies scaling past $50M have a marketing budget. Very few have a marketing identity. The difference between the two is not a creative problem. It is a leadership problem. When your brand cannot survive without a campaign propping it up, growth does not clarify it. Growth exposes it. Here is what culture-led marketing actually looks like at scale, and why it matters before your next funding round or market expansion.
posts.email_subject: What does your brand smell like?

chapters.title: The Talent Engine
chapters.authors: Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein & Yasmin West

articles.title: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring - hrtoday.in
articles.snippet: Ashoka Gurujala Joins 66degrees as Director of Talent Acquisition to Lead AI, Cloud & Data Hiring  hrtoday.in

articles.title: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026 - SHRM
articles.snippet: Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026  SHRM

articles.title: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner - Business Wire
articles.snippet: Jobsity Announces Strategic Acquisition of Eureka Labs, Expanding Scale and Strengthening Its Position as a Leading Nearshore Talent Partner  Business Wire

post1.text:
Hiring more people is not a growth strategy. It is a liability disguised as momentum.

SHRM recently reported that the new standard for hiring in 2026 is precision over scale. Companies winning the talent game are not posting more roles. They are getting ruthlessly specific about which roles actually move the needle.

Here is what that means for leaders scaling past $50M:

1. Every open role should map directly to a revenue or operational outcome. If you cannot draw that line, the role is not ready to fill.

2. Speed-to-hire is the wrong metric. Quality-of-fit in the first 90 days predicts far more about long-term growth than how fast you closed a requisition.

3. Your talent strategy should be rebuilt every 18 months. The skills that got you to $100M are not the skills that get you to $250M.

Most scaling companies are still operating with a headcount mindset when they need a capability mindset.

What is the one role at your company that, if filled perfectly today, would change everything in the next 12 months?

post2.text:
A recent piece in SHRM titled "Precision Over Scale: The New Rules of Hiring in 2026" caught our attention, and it should catch yours too.

The core argument is straightforward: volume hiring is losing ground to targeted, outcome-driven hiring. For companies in the $50M to $500M range, this is not a trend to watch. It is a decision to make right now.

At Autobahn, we see scaling companies stall not because they cannot find talent, but because they hire for today's problems instead of tomorrow's requirements. Building a talent engine that serves where you are going, not where you have been, is one of the clearest separators between companies that plateau and companies that break through.

Read the full article and think about whether your current hiring approach is built for scale or just for speed.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMioAFBVV95cUxPRFVYR0VscXJpMmVqV2dwazJDTkstVDJTNG9uTlZlWHJPVkFqQ0MzZTRQSUYwUDZweWw2dE52THZKSnhBTzQtb1g5UUYzbmJTbjFJcXlVYno2ZzU4aE0yNVRwN2RIN05xTWJVWnZQejVyY2xRNmJ2SF83QmVHel9GYmdWTEdEMWZLMVk5OThMNTF0TmdKR1F4bG84cnZRZ0FU?oc=5

post3.text:
The conversation around precision hiring is everywhere right now, and for good reason. But knowing the principle is not the same as having a system.

Chapter 6 of Rock Your Business, titled "The Talent Engine," goes deeper. Authors Caleb Hawkins, Darla Klein, and Yasmin West lay out what it actually takes to build a people strategy that scales alongside your business, not one that becomes a bottleneck to it.

One thing they make clear early on: the talent decisions that feel urgent rarely align with the talent decisions that are actually important. Scaling companies that confuse the two pay for it later, usually right when they can least afford to.

If your team is your biggest asset, it deserves a real engine behind it, not just a hiring process.

Rock Your Business, the new book from Jonathan and Katherine Slain, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most scaling companies treat hiring as a response to growth. The best ones treat it as the engine of growth. There is a significant difference, and it shows up in your org chart, your culture, and your revenue trajectory. SHRM's latest research confirms what high-growth operators already know: precision beats volume every time. Here is what that actually looks like when you are building past $50M.
posts.email_subject: Why hiring more is slowing you down

chapters.title: Death by KPIs
chapters.authors: Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli & Jim Randall

articles.title: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business - Business.com
articles.snippet: 14 Tools to Track KPIs for Your Business  Business.com

articles.title: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026 - Oracle NetSuite
articles.snippet: 30 Financial Metrics and KPIs to Measure Success in 2026  Oracle NetSuite

articles.title: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance - CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)
articles.snippet: The KPIs to Help Manufacturers Improve Company Performance  CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen)

post1.text:
More KPIs do not mean better performance. In most $50M+ companies, they mean slower decisions and more confusion.

Oracle NetSuite recently catalogued 30 financial metrics worth tracking. Thirty. For most leadership teams, that number is not a dashboard. It is a distraction.

Here is what we see repeatedly with scaling companies:

1. Leaders track what is easy to measure, not what actually drives growth. Vanity metrics crowd out the numbers that would force hard conversations.

2. When every department presents its own KPI stack, executives spend their quarterly reviews reconciling data instead of making calls. The metrics become the meeting.

3. The companies that scale fastest tend to run on five to seven tightly chosen indicators that connect directly to their strategic priorities. Not thirty. Not twenty. Five to seven.

The goal of measurement is decision-making. If your KPI report does not make a decision easier, it is overhead dressed up as accountability.

How many of your current KPIs would survive a ruthless audit?

post2.text:
A recent piece from CLA (CliftonLarsonAllen) outlines the KPIs that help manufacturers sharpen company performance. It is a solid operational framework, and it raises something we talk about with scaling clients constantly: knowing which metrics to track is only half the problem.

The other half is knowing what to do when those numbers move.

Companies growing through the $50M to $500M range often inherit KPI systems built for a smaller, simpler operation. The metrics stay. The context changes. Leadership ends up managing numbers that no longer reflect the business they are actually running.

If your KPI framework has not been pressure-tested against your current growth stage, it is worth the conversation.

Read the full CLA piece here and consider which metrics on that list still fit where your business is headed: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiugFBVV95cUxNc3hGVVVxVnMwVEpLcmxaNWJzaURJbERjczZaSDhlcnhvOFlHX1VZU0tETkctQjFqRVhZVkNtcFNoUWtfSmRhSmU5N3pYc25IVzlfTEs5TjFZWk1RTmhZd2JzSkpPSXVmQndRRHRBM29CaDBoWnVWRXcxMnBhWkcwT3dmWTAwSDFSeS1hYzFtRUNtd05nWE9PX25nbzRfc2ZzSjVFVURMY0hMRkdVMmtOdjVOYUluUXJIcEE

post3.text:
Oracle NetSuite recently published a list of 30 financial metrics and KPIs worth considering for 2026. It is a useful reference. It is also a perfect illustration of the trap that Chapter 7 of our upcoming book addresses head-on.

Chapter 7 is called "Death by KPIs."

Authored by Héctor Arias, Matt Radicelli, and Jim Randall, this chapter was built for leaders who suspect their metrics have multiplied faster than their clarity. The authors make a case that resonates with nearly every company we work with at scale: more measurement does not build more accountability. In many cases, it quietly destroys it.

They offer a different way to think about what belongs on your leadership dashboard, and why the number matters as much as the metric itself.

Rock Your Business, the new book from Autobahn Consultants, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most leadership teams believe they have a metrics problem when they do not have enough data. The real problem is almost always the opposite. Companies scaling past $50M tend to accumulate KPIs the way they accumulate overhead: gradually, and without anyone deciding to. By the time it becomes visible, the dashboard has become the obstacle.
posts.email_subject: Are your KPIs making decisions harder?

articles.title: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era - Harvard Business Review
articles.snippet: Performance Management Needs New Metrics in the AI Era  Harvard Business Review

articles.title: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack - The New York Times
articles.snippet: ‘Talk to My A.I. Twin’: Busy Executives Have a New Productivity Hack  The New York Times

articles.title: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago - Fortune
articles.snippet: Thousands of CEOs admit AI had no impact on employment or productivity—and it has economists resurrecting a paradox from 40 years ago  Fortune

post1.text:
Most companies are measuring AI adoption completely wrong.

The assumption is that AI investment automatically converts to productivity gains. But Fortune recently reported on thousands of CEOs acknowledging that AI has had zero measurable impact on either employment or productivity at their companies. Economists are now revisiting a paradox they thought was buried decades ago.

Here is what leaders scaling past $50M need to understand:

1. Adoption is not the same as integration. Buying tools is not a strategy. AI has to change how decisions get made, not just how tasks get completed.

2. Your metrics are probably still measuring the old way of working. If your KPIs were built before AI entered your workflow, they cannot tell you whether AI is helping.

3. The productivity gap is a leadership problem, not a technology problem. The companies winning with AI have executives who actively redesign workflows around the tools.

The technology is not the bottleneck. Your operating model is.

Are you measuring what AI is actually doing to your business, or just what you spent on it?

post2.text:
A recent piece in The New York Times explored a growing trend among senior executives: deploying AI twins to handle meetings, respond to messages, and represent them in low-stakes interactions.

It reads like a novelty. For scaling companies, it is a signal worth paying attention to.

When your leadership team is the bottleneck, growth stalls. We see this constantly with companies in the $50M to $500M range. Executives are operationally overloaded, and decisions pile up waiting for them.

AI tools that extend executive bandwidth are not a gimmick. They are infrastructure. The leaders who figure out how to delegate to AI thoughtfully will move faster than those still debating whether to try it.

The question is not whether this technology is ready. The question is whether your organization is structured to use it well.

Read the full article here and think about where your leadership team is the constraint: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMifkFVX3lxTE1fZnBYemx0U1pIYUJQSk1WSm1sdThzLWNxcnJmaUNqMDFSajVISUtSXzJBTWFtSW9PY0JjcUVZSjRSaTZwblhuYWg3NUM1Xy1MVmxpSWtHSUJzRmlGZjA1VHBHczEwbkl5UmswVW1qVi00Q0Z3NUtZeHZDb0lEQQ?oc=5

post3.text:
Harvard Business Review is now arguing that performance management needs entirely new metrics for the AI era. We could not agree more, and it is exactly why we dedicated a full chapter to this in our upcoming book.

Chapter 8 of Rock Your Business is called The Third Monitor Theory. Co-authored by Hudson Shank and Jonathan Slain, it tackles one of the most practical questions facing growth-stage companies right now: how do you actually build AI into the way your business operates, not just the way your employees spend an hour on a Tuesday?

The chapter introduces a framework for thinking about AI as a layer of your operating system rather than a collection of tools. One insight that will reframe how you think about productivity: the leaders getting results are not the ones using AI the most. They are the ones using it at the right moments.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Thousands of CEOs just admitted that AI has not moved the needle on productivity at their companies. Before you assume they bought the wrong tools, consider a harder possibility: they never changed how they work. For companies scaling past $50M, AI is not a software decision. It is an operating model decision, and most leadership teams are not having that conversation yet.
posts.email_subject: Why AI isn't working for most CEOs

chapters.title: How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?
chapters.authors: Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins & Rafa Loyo

articles.title: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role - Seattle University
articles.snippet: Six Ways to Overcome Impostor Syndrome in a New Leadership Role  Seattle University

articles.title: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top - Forbes Australia
articles.snippet: Why impostor syndrome follows high achievers all the way to the top  Forbes Australia

articles.title: Don’t call it imposter syndrome - Fast Company
articles.snippet: Don’t call it imposter syndrome  Fast Company

post1.text:
The most dangerous leader in a scaling company is not the one who doubts themselves. It is the one who stopped doubting themselves too soon.

Impostor syndrome gets treated like a weakness to fix. But Forbes Australia recently reported something worth sitting with: the feeling does not go away when you reach the top. High achievers carry it all the way up. That is not a bug. That is data.

Here is what that means for leaders taking companies from $50M to $500M:

1. The discomfort you feel stepping into a bigger role is proportional to how seriously you are taking the job. Comfort at this stage is the real warning sign.

2. Leaders who have made peace with not having all the answers make better decisions under pressure. They ask better questions and build stronger teams around them.

3. Self-doubt, managed well, creates accountability. It keeps you honest with your board, your people, and yourself.

The question is not how to eliminate the feeling. The question is whether you are using it or hiding from it.

What does your relationship with self-doubt actually cost your organization?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fast Company made a sharp argument: stop calling it impostor syndrome.

The label, the article suggests, pathologizes a normal psychological response to high-stakes situations. And that reframing matters more than it might seem.

At Autobahn, we work with leaders scaling companies through the $50M to $500M range. At that stage, the org chart changes, the decisions get bigger, and the margin for error shrinks. Leaders often interpret their anxiety as personal inadequacy when it is actually a rational response to a genuinely harder job.

The companies that scale well are the ones where leaders learn to operate inside that discomfort rather than waiting for it to go away.

Read the full piece and think about how your leadership team is naming what they feel. The language matters.

https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMidkFVX3lxTE9PRnBrU1NWWm1BeEl3dk5qVjFRc0M0T2ctZW9fbjdWd2NmdFlUZVVNeW9JV0VTWkktVmp0MlVjbFVEMXEweWZBS1d6bFBSMVNoNFVfWm5WaFo0aG1PT1RJN0p2Ylc0SkRONnBBcDYzZmNEUUJsRFE

post3.text:
Chapter 9 of Rock Your Business is titled "How Could You Be Successful and Not Have It?" and it asks a question most scaling leaders have never said out loud.

Authored by Samantha Frost, Caleb Hawkins, and Rafa Loyo, the chapter confronts something that does not show up on any financial dashboard: the psychological weight of leading a company through serious growth.

One of the insights that stands out is this. The leaders most likely to stall a company's growth are not the ones who lack skill. They are the ones whose internal narrative has not kept pace with their external results.

There is a gap between what the numbers say and what the leader believes about themselves. Chapter 9 is about closing it.

Rock Your Business, the new book from Jonathan Slain and Katherine Slain, is available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Every leader we work with at the $50M to $500M stage eventually says some version of the same thing: I am not sure I belong in this seat. Most say it privately. A few say it out loud. None of them are wrong to feel it. The ones who scale successfully are not the ones who got rid of the doubt. They are the ones who learned to lead through it anyway.
posts.email_subject: The self-doubt your P&L can't show you

articles.title: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs - Fortune
articles.snippet: Morgan Stanley on life after selling your business: a roadmap for entrepreneurs  Fortune

articles.title: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition - High Point University
articles.snippet: HPU Student Entrepreneurs Earn $30,000 in Business Plan Competition  High Point University

articles.title: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy. - Shopify
articles.snippet: The data says founders would do it again. Even in this economy.  Shopify

post1.text:
The business plan is not the problem. The life plan is.

Most leaders running $50M+ companies have obsessed over financial models, org charts, and growth strategies. Almost none have designed their life with the same rigor. That gap quietly destroys marriages, health, and purpose long before it shows up on a balance sheet.

Morgan Stanley recently noted that entrepreneurs who sell their businesses often struggle more with identity and direction than with finances. The money is there. The meaning is not. That is a planning failure, not a market failure.

Three things scaling leaders almost never do but should:

1. Write down what a great personal life looks like at $500M, not just what a great company looks like.
2. Set non-negotiable personal constraints before the next growth sprint, not after.
3. Treat their own time and energy as a capital allocation decision.

Scaling a company without designing the life around it is just building a faster treadmill.

What does your life plan actually say, and when did you last look at it?

post2.text:
A recent piece in Fortune, drawing on Morgan Stanley research, outlines what happens to entrepreneurs after they sell their businesses. The financial transition gets a roadmap. The personal one rarely does.

At Autobahn, we see this pattern well before the exit. Leaders scaling from $50M to $500M are so locked into the company's next chapter that they stop authoring their own. The result is not burnout in the dramatic sense. It is a slow drift where the business grows and the person inside it quietly shrinks.

The companies that scale well and sustain it tend to have leaders who are intentional about both. Growth strategy and life strategy, built in parallel.

If you are in a serious growth phase right now, ask yourself: does your personal vision for this season match the demands you are signing up for?

Read the full Fortune piece here: https://news.google.com/rss/articles/CBMiiwFBVV95cUxPdnZpWG94aDF5el9jOUtxNWhVYlFXZ3FKejZHQkJEOTVVSmN2VE1COEtTb3lkTnJ1VENGTVoxWGUzOHhYdlJ4QUV6bW9IajBVTEQ2cm5vUWRST3pCcWlxXzNzNHNMQlRmSURudzZZbFNuWkx2YUZWakN5ME1mcVU0Rm9XcFdMOXQzNFo4?oc=5

post3.text:
Shopify recently published data showing that most founders would do it all over again, even in a difficult economy. What the data does not show is how many of them would design their personal lives differently the second time around.

Chapter 10 of Rock Your Business, titled Coming Up for Air, tackles exactly that tension. Written by Samantha Frost alongside Jonathan and Katherine Slain, the chapter challenges leaders to treat life planning with the same seriousness they bring to business planning.

One idea from the chapter that tends to stop people cold: the version of you that gets the company to $500M may not be the version your family or your health can afford to maintain indefinitely. There is a design question underneath the growth question, and most leaders never stop long enough to ask it.

Rock Your Business, available soon. Follow us for updates.

posts.blog_hook: Most $50M+ leaders have a five-year plan for their company and no plan at all for their life. That is not a scheduling problem. It is a strategy problem. The same rigor that drives revenue growth, applied to how you actually want to live, changes everything about how you scale and whether you survive it intact.
posts.email_subject: Your business has a plan. Does your life?
